title: Shona History - Oral Tradition of Sekuru Tute Chigamba
author: Sekuru Tute Chigamba (as told to Jennifer Kyker)
description: The oral history of the Shona people as told by Zimbabwe mbira player, oral historian, and ritual specialist Sekuru Tute Chigamba. This scroll traces the migration from the north (Iraq/Iran region) through Egypt, Ethiopia, Tanzania, to Zimbabwe, and explains the origins of totems, names, and the Rozvi dynasty.
source_attribution: Oral history collected by Jennifer Kyker from Sekuru Tute Chigamba

verses.text: According to oral tradition, Murenga came from the north - from somewhere around Iraq or Iran. He left there together with his family and children. In his place of origin, he had the name SOKO. He was called Soko.

After leaving the north, Murenga eventually arrived in Egypt. When he entered Egypt, the Egyptians tried to fight him. As he was a powerful person, he wanted to protect his family. He thought perhaps his children would be killed.

That is when Murenga leaped up. There was an Egyptian mounted on a camel, holding a white spear. Murenga leaped up, wrested the spear away, and began to fight the Egyptians. All the Egyptians ran away, crying 'This person is God himself!' So there they called him MURENGA, meaning 'God.' His children gave him the name PFUMOJENA, meaning 'White Spear.'

After being named Pfumojena, Murenga departed from Egypt. He began migrating south. But he never arrived in Guruuswa (Tanganyika). He died on the way, before they had arrived. Murenga's children are the ones who made it to Guruuswa.

After arriving in Guruuswa, they turned back around again. They went to Ethiopia, where they erected MASVINGO - buildings of stone. There are various masvingo that they built there, and that is where their king lived.

From Ethiopia they returned to Arusha. Their descendants arrived in Guruuswa and settled there. Then they said, 'Let us journey onward and see how things are further ahead.' Some stayed in Guruuswa, others migrated following the coast, and still others came straight down the middle.

When those who came down the middle arrived at the Zambezi River, they found it was full. They said, 'We are not capable of fording these waters. What shall we do?' That is when BIRI took her cloth, hit the water, and it parted, and they crossed. As they were exiting, the waters joined back together. And that is how they arrived in Zimbabwe.

The voice of Mwari spoke at Matonjeni, called Mabwe aDziva, just beyond Bulawayo. When you arrive at the cave you all sit together. There are not many totems - there is only one totem, SOKO. When you enter the cave there you are all Soko. That voice ended up at Mabwe aDziva.

That voice - when something troubled you, you would simply board a bus and go there. You would arrive, see it was full of people, and sit down. The voice would say, 'You are the child of so-and-so, you have come here for this reason. What is troubling you is as follows, and you must do as follows.' You would return and do what you were told. But these days, people have erred, and the voice no longer speaks.

In Masvingo there is another place where not just anyone was permitted to go. Inside the stone walls, there is a place that should not be visited - that is where the voice of Mwari, or God, was supposed to dwell. Now they have desecrated that place. The whites are mainly responsible, because they made it a tourist attraction. They didn't know the commandments that the people who lived there followed.

Upon entering Zimbabwe, they found only birds and people. There were no animals. Animals followed afterward, trailing behind Soko's children. Animals arrived here after people, but birds preceded them.

When they arrived, they found short people - the Khoisan. The Shona called them MANDIONEREPI, meaning 'Where do you see me?' Because they would ask, 'Where do you see me?' And if you said 'I see you are right here,' they would fight you - they didn't want to be considered short people. If you said 'I see you are all the way over there,' they would call you a good person.

The Khoisan were hunters who ate honey and meat. Those who came next were agriculturalists who grew finger millet, which we call NJERA or ZVIYO. That was their primary crop and the type of sadza eaten here. They brought njera with them on their migration. Those who stayed behind in Ethiopia, Mali, Somalia, and Eritrea all have njera. That is the beer of the vadzimu ancestral spirits - doro remukono.

After they entered Zimbabwe, they settled and encountered others who had taken different migration routes. Those who followed the coast are called MAHUNGWE - people of the fish eagle (hungwe). All the way from Rusape to Inyanga, they are called Mahungwe.

Once people settled, they constructed buildings out of stone - MASVINGO. They said, 'This is our great city, where we shall henceforth dwell. Our king shall live inside, ruling over his domain. And this is where we shall pray to our ancestral spirits.'

There was a bird, a type of fish eagle called NJERERE, that lived at Masvingo. If anything bad was going to happen, it would cry all night long. That fish eagle's image continues to appear on all things related to Zimbabwe - on the flag. It is a great bird because it sat atop Masvingo's great stone walls.

Those who built Masvingo said, 'We shall all live together with our king.' But they didn't realize how their numbers would grow. As their population increased, it caused them to commit crimes of incest (makunakuna). So Masvingo fell, and people began to move in various directions until they had all dispersed.

They returned to DZIVARASEKWA - called so because that is where they first started giving each other names. During their migration they didn't call each other by name, they simply said 'You!' The only name they had was Soko, Pfumojena, or Murenga. Eventually they started giving each other names until names were common across the entire country.

There was a tree with bees in it. One said, 'What if we took a long pole to get at the honey?' Another laughed and said, 'You are CHINGOO (Long pole).' One said, 'The fire has gotten wet.' They said, 'You are MUTOTA (One who is wet).' One said, 'I have eaten the honey!' They said, 'You are MUROZVI (One who takes what is not his).' So they gave names according to what people did or said.

MUROZVI was declared CHIGOVANYIKA - the allocator of territories. Today he would be called the Minister of Land. His hwize (chiefly regalia) was an axe, and he used it to demarcate territorial boundaries. He knew the boundary of every person's territory. The Rozvi ruled from Zimbabwe to Zambia - all of Barotseland belongs to the Rozvi. Luwanika was a Rozvi who left here and settled in Zambia.

As descendants grew in number, totems were established. Some adopted totems based on people's names. Some kept SOKO, their father's name. Some are MOYO (heart) - in Shona, vamoyo means a child close to your heart, a daughter. There is a proverb: 'He who has cultivated four anthills has gained nothing' - one who has borne daughters has gained nothing because all will be married. But if you bear daughters you have planted beans - wealth will come to you.

When you adopt a totem, you identify an animal to taboo which you do not eat. Those of MOYO do not eat heart. Those of SOKO do not eat monkey. SHAVA taboo the eland (mhofu). NYATI taboo the buffalo. SHUMBA taboo the lion. TEMBO taboo the zebra. NHEWA taboo the leopard (mbada). SAUNYAMA taboo the python (shato). MUSONI taboo a tiny mouse with three dark stripes.

After people scattered from Masvingo, they built another stone complex near Gweru called Chitakochemhuka where King MUTOTA lived. His in-laws, the Soko people, moved to Guruve. The king went to live with his in-laws in Guruve. He was given the entire valley from the Manyame River to the Musengezi River, up to the Kazi River - all of DANDEMUTANDE.

Once Mutota settled there, he was no longer called Moyo - his totem became SOKO CHIDZIWO (guardian of the boundary). His son Kasekete came with the king's sister to Muzarabani. They were given land by people of the elephant totem (Nzou). Kasekete became NZOU SAMANYANGA. Though father and son now had different totems, going further back their true totem is MOYO.

Three of Mutota's children went to Mozambique. One went to Malawi, one went to Goa in India about four hundred years ago - the first person to learn Portuguese. The last to rule under Mutota's kingship was MAPONDERA. Mapondera was made king, and he fought with the whites as they were arriving. He caused them a lot of trouble. He was the last to exercise Mutota's rule.
